1: hēn lĕṣedeq yimlōk melek = "Behold, a king (melek) will reign (mālaḵ) in righteousness (ṣedeq)" — the messianic-political ideal: a king who is characterized by ṣedeq (righteousness = conformity to divine standard of justice). wĕlĕmišpāṭ śārîm yāśōrû = "and princes will rule (śārar) in justice (mišpāṭ)." The pairing ṣedeq/mišpāṭ = the twin pillars of the restored kingdom (cf. Isaiah 1:26-27; Isaiah 9:7; Isaiah 11:4-5; Isaiah 33:5). The righteous king anticipates the Davidic Messiah who will embody both.
2: wĕhāyâ hāʾîš kĕmastēr-rûaḥ ûmahseh zerem = "And that man will be like a shelter from the wind (rûaḥ) and a refuge from the rainstorm (zerem)" — four natural refuge images: shelter from wind, shelter from storm, streams of water in a dry place, shadow of a great rock in a parched land. Each describes the protective function of the righteous king for the vulnerable. The rock in a weary land recalls the water-from-the-rock of the wilderness (Exod 17; Num 20) and anticipates Paul's identification of that rock as Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4). kĕpalgê-mayim bĕṣāyôn kĕṣēl sela kāḇēd bĕʾereṣ ʿāyēpâ = "like streams of water in a dry place, like the shadow of a great rock in a weary land."
3: wĕlōʾ tišʿênâ ʿênê rōʾîm = "and the eyes of those who see will not be closed (šāʿâ)" — the reversal of the hardening commission of Isaiah 6:9-10 (where eyes were blinded and ears made heavy). In the kingdom of the righteous king, the perceptual faculties that were sealed in judgment are reopened. wĕʾoznê šōmĕʿîm tiqšābnâ = "and the ears of those who hear will pay attention (qāšaḇ)" — qāšaḇ = to incline toward, to attend carefully. The attentiveness that Israel refused (Isaiah 1:2; Isaiah 28:23) will characterize the restored community.
4: wĕlēḇāḇ mahîrîm yāḇîn lādaʿat = "and the heart of the hasty (mahîrîm) will understand to know (yāḇîn lādaʿat)" — those who were rash in judgment will gain measured understanding. ûlĕšôn ʿilgîm tĕmahēr lĕdabbēr ṣāḥôt = "and the tongue of the stammerers (ʿilgîm) will speak clearly (ṣāḥôt)" — the stammerer whose speech was unclear will speak plainly and readily. All four verses (vv3-4) describe the restoration of communicative capacity — seeing, hearing, understanding, speaking — in the kingdom of the righteous king.
5: lōʾ-yiqqārēʾ ʿôd lĕnāḇāl nāḏîḇ = "the fool (nāḇāl) will no longer be called noble (nāḏîḇ)" — the social reversal of titles: the morally bankrupt person who held elite status will no longer be honored. ûlĕkilay lōʾ yēʾāmer šôaʿ = "and the deceiver (kilay) will not be called generous (šôaʿ)" — the exploitation labeled as generosity will be exposed. The righteous king's reign brings truth in naming: people will be called what they are, not what they have fraudulently claimed.
6: kî nāḇāl nĕḇālâ yĕdabbēr = "for the fool speaks folly (nĕḇālâ)" — nāḇāl (H5036) = the fool as a moral category (cf. Psalms 14:1: "the fool says in his heart, there is no God"), not merely an intellectual classification. wĕlibbô yaʿăśeh-ʾāwen lĕʿăśôt ḥānēp = "and his heart practices iniquity (ʾāwen), to practice godlessness (ḥānep)." wĕdabbēr ʾel-YHWH tôʿâ = "and he speaks error concerning YHWH" — theological confusion at the root of social evil. lĕhārēq nepeš rāʿēḇ = "to leave the hungry soul (nepeš) empty" — the practical consequence of foolishness: the hungry go unfed. The fool's theological error produces social neglect.
7: wĕkilay kĕlāyw rāʿîm = "and the deceiver — his weapons (kĕlîm) are evil" — kĕlîm literally = implements/weapons; the deceiver's instruments of action are evil from the start. hûʾ zim-môt yaʿaṣ lĕḥabbēl ʿănāwîm bĕʾimrê-šāqer = "he devises wicked schemes to ruin the poor (ʿănāwîm) with false words (ʾimrê-šāqer)" — the target of the deceiver's evil is consistently the vulnerable (ʿănāwîm, the lowly/poor). wĕʾeḇyôn bĕdabbēr mišpāṭ = "and the needy (ʾeḇyôn) when he speaks what is right" — the needy person seeking legal justice is the particular victim of the deceiver's false words.
8: wĕnāḏîḇ nĕḏîḇôt yaʿăṣ wĕhûʾ ʿal-nĕḏîḇôt yāqûm = "but the noble person (nāḏîḇ) devises noble things (nĕḏîḇôt), and by noble things he stands." The contrast to the fool (vv6-7) and the deceiver: the truly noble person is characterized by noble intentions that match their noble status. Nāḏîḇ in the restored kingdom means what it should mean: those with elevated status use it for elevated purposes, not for personal enrichment at the expense of the poor.
9: šaʾănannôt bānôt šimʿû qôlî bānôt bôṭĕḥôt haʾăzinnâ ʾimrātî = "Rise up, you women who are at ease (šaʾănannôt), hear my voice; you complacent daughters, listen to my speech" — the address to women of leisure echoes Isaiah 3:16-4:1 (the indictment of the daughters of Zion). Here the complacent women represent the ease of those who assume security without righteousness. The prophetic address to women signals a particular social class: those insulated from the consequences of political decisions by wealth.
10: yāmîm ʿal-šānâ tirgazânâ bôṭĕḥôt = "within a year and some days (yāmîm ʿal-šānâ), you will be in anguish, complacent ones" — a specific time indicator, unusual in prophetic oracles: the complacency will be shattered within a year. kî-kālâ bāṣîr ʾāsip bĕlî yāḇôʾ = "for the vintage (bāṣîr) will fail, the harvest (ʾāsip) will not come" — the agricultural metaphor for the economic collapse that will follow Assyrian invasion. The women's comfortable life depends on agricultural abundance that is about to end.
11: ḥirĕdû šaʾănannôt rĕgāzû bôṭĕḥôt = "Tremble, you women who are at ease; quake, complacent ones" — the reversal: the at-ease women will tremble (ḥārad), the self-secure will quake (rāgaz). Pitšĕṭû wĕʿôr ûḥigîrû ʿal-ḥălāṣāyim = "strip yourselves bare and put on sackcloth (śaq) on your loins" — the movement from fine clothing (Isaiah 3:16-24) to sackcloth is the outward sign of mourning, the reversal of the adornments described in Isaiah 3:18-23.
12: ʿal-šādayim sōpĕdîm = "beat your breasts (šādayim)" — the gesture of grief: beating the chest is the universal mourning posture in the ancient Near East (cf. Luke 18:13 where the tax collector beats his breast; Luke 23:48 where crowds beat their breasts after the crucifixion). ʿal śĕdê ḥemdâ ʿal-gepen pōriyyâ = "for the pleasant fields, for the fruitful vine" — the mourning is specifically over lost agricultural abundance: the good land destroyed by invasion.
13: ʿal ʾadmat ʿammî qôṣ šāmîr taʿăleh = "Over the soil (ʾadmâ) of my people thorns (qôṣ) and briars (šāmîr) shall come up" — the šāmîr wāšît pair (Isaiah 5:6; Isaiah 7:23-25) signals the curse-reversal of the land; the cultivated soil returns to wilderness. kî ʿal-kol-bāttê māśôś qiryat ʿallîzâ = "over all the joyful houses in the exultant city" — Jerusalem, characterized by festive houses and joyful celebration, will become a desolation of thorns.
14: kî-ʾarmon nuṭṭāš hāmôn ʿîr ʿuzZāḇ = "for the palace (ʾarmôn) will be forsaken (nāṭaš), the noisy city (hāmôn ʿîr) abandoned (ʿāzaḇ)" — the architectural symbols of urban civilization — palace and city — emptied. ʿōpel ûḇaḥan hāyû bĕʿad mĕʿārâ ʿad-ʿôlām = "the hill and the watchtower will become dens (mĕʿārâ) forever (ʿad-ʿôlām)" — permanent reversal: the defensive infrastructure becomes animal shelter. śimḥat pĕrāʾîm = "the delight of wild donkeys" — the city that was a delight to humans becomes a pasture for feral animals.
15: ʿad-yiqqāreh ʿālênû rûaḥ mimārome = "until the Spirit (rûaḥ) is poured upon us from on high (mimārome)" — the great turning point: the Spirit poured from above. Yiqqāreh (niphal of ʿārâ) = to be poured out. This is one of the great OT Spirit-promise texts: the Spirit poured from heaven transforming the earth (cf. Ezekiel 36:26-27; Isaiah 37:14; Joel 2:28-29). wĕhāyâ midbar lakarmel wĕhakarmel layaʿar yeḥāšēḇ = "and the wilderness becomes a fruitful field (karmel) and the fruitful field is regarded as a forest." The outpouring of the Spirit produces the agricultural reversal: desert → garden → forest. Acts 2 (the Spirit poured at Pentecost) is the initial fulfillment; the consummation remains eschatological.
16: wĕšākan bammidbar mišpāṭ = "then justice (mišpāṭ) will dwell (šākan) in the wilderness" — šākan = to tabernacle, dwell as a resident; the same root as the Mishkan (tabernacle) and the divine dwelling. Justice tabernacles in what was previously wilderness — the Spirit's transforming work is both ecological and social. ûṣĕdāqâ bakkarmel tēšēḇ = "and righteousness (ṣĕdāqâ) will abide (yāšaḇ) in the fruitful field." Both pillars of the ideal kingdom (v1: ṣedeq/mišpāṭ) are now embedded in the transformed landscape: the Spirit-created ecology is also the Spirit-created social order.
17: wĕhāyâ maʿăśēh haṣṣĕdāqâ šālôm = "and the work (maʿăśeh) of righteousness will be peace (šālôm)" — righteousness produces peace as its necessary fruit. waʿăḇōdat haṣṣĕdāqâ hašqēṭ ûḇeṭaḥ ʿad-ʿôlām = "and the service of righteousness will be quietness and trust (beṭaḥ) forever." Hašqēṭ ûḇeṭaḥ echoes Isaiah 30:15 ("in quietness and trust is your strength") — the faith-rest that Israel refused becomes the permanent condition of the restored community under the Spirit. Peace as the fruit of righteousness, not its precondition.
18: wĕyāšaḇ ʿammî bĕnĕwēh šālôm = "and my people will dwell in a peaceful habitation (nĕwēh šālôm)" — nĕwēh = pasture, home, settled dwelling place; the image of the flock in its safe pasture applied to the human community. ûḇĕmiškĕnôt miṭṭāḥôt = "and in secure dwellings (miškĕnôt miṭṭāḥôt)" — miškān (tabernacle-root) = dwelling place; miṭṭāḥ = spreading out, relaxed security. ûḇimnûḥôt šaʾănannôt = "and in quiet resting places" — the three terms stack up into a comprehensive image of shalom: peaceful place, secure dwelling, quiet rest. The "complacent women" (vv9-12) will have true security rather than false security.
19: ûbārad bĕredet hāyāʿar = "and hail will fall (bārad) when the forest is brought low" — the judgment on the proud (the forest as symbol of arrogant power, cf. Isaiah 10:18-19; Isaiah 29:17) comes through hail, a weapon of theophanic warfare (cf. Isaiah 30:30). ûšiplâ hāʿîr tišpāl = "and the city (hāʿîr) will be utterly humbled (šāpēl)" — the city of human pride is brought low by divine hail. The verse interrupts the peace-oracle (vv15-18) with a reminder: the shalom follows judgment, not instead of it.
20: ʾašrêkem zorĕʿê ʿal-kol-māyim = "Blessed (ʾašrê) are you who sow beside all waters" — the final beatitude of the chapter: those who farm the Spirit-watered land are blessed. mĕšallĕḥê regel-hašŝôr wĕhaḥămôr = "who let the feet of the ox and donkey roam free" — the animals graze freely in the restored land. The chapter that began with the righteous king (v1) ends with the blessed farmer: the kingdom of righteousness produces not only justice in the courts but abundance in the fields. The Spirit poured from above (v15) results in land, livestock, and human flourishing.
